narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 63.9% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic and background factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances, while setting aside demographics like gender or race. In your case, the model was most swayed by your ranking among peers in your third-year grades, which stood out as the strongest signal pushing toward a higher risk of bar failure, since it placed you in the bottom decile compared to where successful bar takers typically landed around the sixth decile. Your first-year grade decile also contributed notably in the same direction, reinforcing concerns because it was similarly low relative to the averages for those who passed. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA provided some counterbalance, pulling the prediction slightly toward success as it aligned better with passing students' profiles, though not enough to offset the other factors. Overall, these early academic rankings carried the most weight in tipping the scales toward failure risk, more so than elements like your LSAT score of 27, which was below the 37 average for bar passers, or your upper-middle family income bracket that matches a common profile among successful students. Being full-time aligns well with the vast majority of those who passed, but it wasn't enough to shift the outcome. Compared to typical admitted students, who often enter with stronger year-one and year-three deciles around the 5th to 6th percentiles and higher undergrad GPAs near 3.24, your profile signaled a tougher path to bar success based on historical trends. This isn't a judgment on your potential or effort—many students overcome similar starts through targeted improvement. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to 37 or above, strengthening your undergrad record if possible through post-bac courses, and seeking academic advising to climb those grade deciles in future studies. Reapplying after addressing these key areas could significantly improve your odds, as the model responds strongly to upward trends in early performance metrics. You're capable of changing this trajectory—many have.
